_meta.description: Inverse Document Frequency glossary for Blood Meridian → Ancient Greek translation

conventions.marking_policy: Words tagged modern_loan or transliteration MUST be visually distinguished in output: use *italics* in markdown, \textit{} in LaTeX, or <i> in HTML. This signals to the reader that the word is foreign to the ancient register.

the_kid.justification: παῖς covers both 'child' and 'young man' in classical and Koine usage. Korto alternates ο μικρός (colloquial) and το παιδί; we unify to παῖς which has the right ambiguity — child/servant/youth. Avoids νεανίσκος (too specific to 'young man') and παιδίον (too diminutive).

the_judge.justification: κριτής over δικαστής. In the Septuagint, κριτής = 'judge' in the Book of Judges — figures of terrifying divine-adjacent authority, not merely courtroom officials. This captures the judge's cosmic, quasi-biblical stature. δικαστής is too Athenian-institutional.

reverend_green.justification: ἱερεύς = 'priest' broadly. Green is a fraudulent preacher, not truly ordained, but the narrative calls him 'reverend'. Γρῖνος hellenises the name (cf. how Greek historians hellenise foreign names). Alternative: ὁ ψευδοπροφήτης for later ironic references.
reverend_green.notes: Γρῖνος is a hellenisation, not a transliteration — no marking needed

holden.justification: Hellenised transliteration following the pattern of Greek historians rendering barbarian names (cf. Ξέρξης, Δαρεῖος). Ὅλδην with rough breathing echoes the English 'H'. Usually referred to as ὁ κριτής; full name used sparingly.
holden.notes: Mark in italics when the transliterated form appears

toadvine.justification: Hellenised with Greek masculine ending -ος. The name is too culturally specific to translate (toad + vine).

glanton.justification: Hellenised with -ων ending (cf. Πλάτων, Κρίτων). The ντ cluster is natural in Greek.

the_delawares.justification: Ethnonym, transliterated with Greek plural ending. Following the pattern of how Greek authors render foreign tribal names (cf. οἱ Μασσαγέται).

the_comanche.justification: Ethnonym with hellenised plural. Could alternatively use a descriptive epithet (οἱ ἱππεῖς τῆς ἐρήμου = 'horsemen of the desert') in elevated passages.

pistol.justification: No classical equivalent exists. Hellenised with neuter diminutive ending -ιον (cf. βιβλίον). MUST be marked in output. Alternative periphrasis for elevated passages: τὸ μικρὸν πυροβόλον ('small fire-thrower').

rifle.justification: πυροβόλον = 'fire-thrower', a transparent compound from πῦρ + βάλλω. Used in Byzantine Greek for firearms. Preferable to a modern loanword for the primary weapon. In elevated narrative, τὸ ὅπλον (generic 'weapon') may suffice where context is clear.
rifle.notes: πυροβόλον is a Greek compound, not a loan — no marking needed

gunpowder.justification: πυρῖτις = 'fire-substance' (from πῦρ). Attested in Byzantine texts for gunpowder. κόνις ('dust/powder') can be added for clarity. Avoids the Turkish loanword μπαρούτι.

gunshot_shooting.justification: Verbal form of πυροβόλον. Transparent Greek compound. Byzantine attestation.

knife.justification: Standard classical word. Also covers 'short sword' — appropriate for the bowie knives in BM.

bowie_knife.ancient_greek: ἡ μεγάλη μάχαιρα / τὸ κυνηγετικὸν ξίφος
bowie_knife.justification: Korto uses 'hunting knife' (κυνηγετικό μαχαίρι). In ancient Greek: μεγάλη μάχαιρα ('great knife') or κυνηγετικὸν ξίφος ('hunting sword'). The size distinction matters in BM.

scalp_scalping.ancient_greek: τὸ δέρμα τῆς κεφαλῆς (noun) / ἀποδέρω τὴν κεφαλήν (verb)
scalp_scalping.justification: Periphrasis: 'the skin of the head' / 'to flay the head'. Herodotus (4.64) describes Scythian scalping using ἀποδέρω — this is our strongest classical precedent and McCarthy would approve.
scalp_scalping.notes: Herodotus 4.64 is the locus classicus for scalping in Greek literature

desert.justification: ἔρημος is the Septuagintal desert par excellence. Carries the right biblical desolation. ἐρημία for 'desolation' as abstract quality.

plain_prairie.justification: Standard classical word for flat ground, plain. Works for prairies and mesas alike in non-specific usage.

mesa.ancient_greek: ἡ τράπεζα (τοῦ ὄρους) / τὸ ἐπίπεδον ὄρος
mesa.justification: τράπεζα = 'table' — a calque of the Spanish 'mesa'. Alternatively τὸ ἐπίπεδον ὄρος = 'flat-topped mountain'.

mud.justification: πηλός = mud, clay (also used in Genesis for the clay of creation — resonant with BM's 'another kind of clay'). ἰλύς = slime, mire, for wetter/nastier contexts.

blood.justification: Universal, unchanged from Homer to Koine. The title word.

dust.justification: Classical. Also 'ashes' connotation which suits BM's landscape.

stars.justification: Standard. The Leonid meteor shower passage is important — ἀστέρες πίπτοντες ('falling stars').

mule.justification: Standard classical. Literally 'half-ass'. Well attested in Homer and prose.

tavern_saloon.justification: καπηλεῖον = 'wine-shop, tavern'. Well attested in comedy and prose. Captures the low-status drinking establishment. Avoids transliterated σαλούν.

hotel.justification: πανδοκεῖον = 'inn' (Luke 10:34, the Good Samaritan). The NT resonance is appropriate — these are rough frontier inns, not modern hotels.

whore_prostitute.justification: Direct continuity from ancient to modern Greek. NT usage (Matthew, Revelation) gives it the right register for BM's biblical-realist tone.

pilgrim.justification: Korto's choice works perfectly in ancient Greek too. A rare case of MG→AG direct transfer.

dollar_money.justification: ἀργύριον = 'silver, money' — generic and timeless. Never translate 'dollar' literally. στατήρ for individual coins where needed.

hat.justification: πέτασος = wide-brimmed hat (traveller's hat, as worn by Hermes). πῖλος = felt cap. Use πέτασος for the wide-brimmed hats that dominate BM; πῖλος for close-fitting caps.
hat.notes: πέτασος is perfect for the wide-brim hats — it even looks like a cowboy hat

boots.justification: ἐμβάδες = heavy boots, marching boots (Xenophon). κρηπῖδες = half-boots. Use ἐμβάδες as default — these are rough frontier boots.

coat.ancient_greek: ὁ χιτών (coat) / τὸ κηρωτὸν ἱμάτιον (oilcloth slicker)
coat.justification: χιτών for generic coat/shirt. For oilcloth slicker: κηρωτὸν ἱμάτιον = 'waxed garment' — a descriptive compound. Sailors waxed their cloaks in antiquity.

shirt.justification: λίνεος χιτών = 'linen tunic'. McCarthy's 'thin and ragged linen shirt' → λίνεος χιτὼν λεπτὸς καὶ ῥακώδης.

nacogdoches.justification: Hellenised with Greek plural/place ending. Following Herodotean practice of rendering foreign toponyms into Greek phonology.

fort_smith.justification: φρούριον = 'fort, stronghold' (classical). Name in genitive: 'the fort of Smith'. Only the proper name is marked.
fort_smith.notes: φρούριον is classical Greek — only *Σμίθου needs marking

memphis.justification: Memphis already exists in Greek — it's the Egyptian city name. The coincidence is a gift.

new_orleans.justification: Νέα is native Greek ('new'). Only Ὀρλεάνη is foreign. Could also use Νέα Αὐρηλιανή (from French 'Orléans' < Latin Aurelianum) for a more deeply hellenised form.

texas.justification: Indeclinable transliteration. Too well-known to hellenise further.

saint_louis.justification: Ἅγιος is native Greek. Λουδοβῖκος is the hellenised form of Louis/Ludwig (used in Byzantine texts). More elegant than raw transliteration.
saint_louis.notes: Only Λουδοβῖκος needs marking — Ἅγιος is Greek

blood_meridian.english: Blood Meridian, or The Evening Redness in the West
blood_meridian.korto_mg: Ματωμένος Μεσημβρινός, ή Το Κόκκινο του Δειλινού στη Δύση
blood_meridian.ancient_greek: Ὁ Αἱματόεις Μεσημβρινός, ἢ Τὸ Ἑσπέριον Ἐρύθημα
blood_meridian.justification: αἱματόεις = 'bloody' (Homeric, poetic). μεσημβρινός = 'meridian/of midday' (already Greek in origin). ἑσπέριον ἐρύθημα = 'western redness' — ἕσπερος = 'evening/western', ἐρύθημα = 'redness'. Korto's Ματωμένος Μεσημβρινός is literally 'Bloodied Meridian'.
blood_meridian.notes: The title is all native Greek — no marking needed
